request.question: What is the nationality of this professional boxer who lost to Breidis Prescott in a upset match in 2008 that was heavily favoured toward him?

title: 1929–30 NHL season
Daulis Prescott
1954 FIFA World Cup Final
Breidis Prescott
1993 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season
Jorge Rubio (boxing trainer)
104th Grey Cup
Amir Khan (boxer)
Federico Gaio
1982–83 Quebec Nordiques season
sentences: The 1929–30 NHL season was the 13th season of the National Hockey League.
Ten teams played 44 games each.
The Montreal Canadiens upset the heavily favoured Boston Bruins two games to none for the Stanley Cup.
Daulis Prescott Consuegra (born 2 September 1986) is a Colombian professional boxer who has challenged once for the WBA featherweight title in 2012.
His brother Breidis Prescott is also a professional boxer.
The 1954 FIFA World Cup Final was the final match of the 1954 FIFA World Cup, the fifth World Cup in FIFA history.
The game was played at the Wankdorf Stadium in Bern, Switzerland, on 4 July 1954, and saw West Germany beat the heavily favoured Golden Team of Hungary 3–2.
Breidis Enrique Prescott Consuegra (born May 3, 1983), best known as Breidis Prescott, is a Colombian professional boxer.
In his early career he had a reputation as a fearsome puncher, winning 18 of his first 20 fights by knockout.
In 2008, Prescott first became known on the world stage when he upset heavily favoured and then-undefeated Olympian Amir Khan, which gave rise to Prescott's nickname of "The Khanqueror".
The 1993 Hamilton Tiger-Cats season was the 36th season for the team in the Canadian Football League and their 44th overall.
The Tiger-Cats finished in 2nd place in the East division with a 6–12–0 record.
After defeating the Ottawa Rough Riders in the East Semi-Final, the team appeared in the East Final, but lost to the heavily favoured Winnipeg Blue Bombers by a single point.
Jorge Enrique Rubio is a Cuban trainer in the sport of boxing.
Rubio was a trainer of the Cuban national amateur boxing team.
Rubio trains numerous professional fighters in Miami, Florida, including Gil Reyes, Juan Arroyo, and Stacey Reile.
Rubio was announced as Amir Khan's trainer on July 26, 2008.
After Khan's disastrous first-round KO defeat at the hands of relatively unknown Colombian Breidis Prescott, Khan and Rubio parted ways.

The 104th Grey Cup (branded as the 104th Grey Cup presented by Shaw for sponsorship reasons) was a Canadian football game that was played on November 27, 2016, between the Calgary Stampeders and the Ottawa Redblacks, that decided the champion for the 2016 CFL season.
In an upset, Ottawa defeated the heavily favoured Stampeders 39–33 in overtime to win a championship in just their third season of existence.
This was the third Grey Cup game to go into overtime (the other two instances came in 1961 and 2005).

This also marked a first that a CFL team won its division with a losing record (8-9-1) and became the 3rd worst team (behind the 2000 BC Lions and 2001 Calgary Stampeders who both went 8-10) to win the Grey Cup.
Amir Iqbal Khan (born 8 December 1986) is a British professional boxer.
He is a former unified light-welterweight world champion, having held the WBA (later Super) title from 2009 to 2012, and the IBF title in 2011.
Additionally he held the Commonwealth lightweight title from 2007 to 2008, the WBC Silver welterweight title from 2014 to 2016, and has challenged once for a middleweight world title in 2016.
Federico Gaio (born 5 March 1992 in Faenza, Italy) is an Italian professional tennis player.
He competes mainly in the Challenger Tour and ITF circuit.
He has seven ITF singles titles and three ITF doubles titles.

He reached his career high singles ranking of World No. 158 in August 2016 after winning his maiden singles challenger title, defeating the heavily favoured Tomasz Bellucci 76, 62 at the Biella Challenger in Italy
The 1982–83 Quebec Nordiques season was the Nordiques fourth season in the National Hockey League.

In the 1981–82 season, Quebec recorded their first winning season in the NHL, as they had a record of 33-31-16, earning 82 points, in which they finished fourth in the Adams Division, qualifying for the post-season for the second consecutive season.

The Nordiques upset the heavily favoured Montreal Canadiens and Boston Bruins to represent the Adams Division in the Wales Conference finals, however, the New York Islanders swept Quebec in the Conference finals, preventing the club from making the Stanley Cup finals.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: What is the nationality of this professional boxer who lost to Breidis Prescott in a upset match in 2008 that was heavily favoured toward him?
hop1_docs: Breidis Prescott | Breidis Enrique Prescott Consuegra (born May 3, 1983), best known as Breidis Prescott, is a Colombian professional boxer.  In his early career he had a reputation as a fearsome puncher, winning 18 of his first 20 fights by knockout.  In 2008, Prescott first became known on the world stage when he upset heavily favoured and then-undefeated Olympian Amir Khan, which gave rise to Prescott's nickname of "The Khanqueror".

Daulis Prescott | Daulis Prescott Consuegra (born 2 September 1986) is a Colombian professional boxer who has challenged once for the WBA featherweight title in 2012.  His brother Breidis Prescott is also a professional boxer.

Jorge Rubio (boxing trainer) | Jorge Enrique Rubio is a Cuban trainer in the sport of boxing.  Rubio was a trainer of the Cuban national amateur boxing team.  Rubio trains numerous professional fighters in Miami, Florida, including Gil Reyes, Juan Arroyo, and Stacey Reile.  Rubio was announced as Amir Khan's trainer on July 26, 2008.  After Khan's disastrous first-round KO defeat at the hands of relatively unknown Colombian Breidis Prescott, Khan and Rubio parted ways.

Charles Manyuchi | Charles Manyuchi is a Zimbabwean professional boxer who is the WBC, international welterweight champion since March 2014.  In November 2014, he successfully defended his WBC crown in a Lusaka, Zambia fight against an opponent from Colombia.  In 2014 he was awarded the Zimbabwe's Sportsperson of the year accolade.  In July 2015 against an Italian boxer in Friuli-Venezia Giulia, he defended his crown successfully.In 2017 saw the fall of great man who was bound for glory and wider recognition internationally when he lost his title to Quadratilo from Singapore in what was referred as a huge upset in the boxing world especially to the fans back home.  Coming back home,he received unwelcome remarks and comments from many fans but he however had support from government claiming to sue those who continued to attack him.  The are currently plans to have a rematch.

Leon Spinks | Leon Spinks (born July 11, 1953) is an American former professional boxer who competed from 1977 to 1995.  In only his eighth professional fight, he won the undisputed heavyweight championship in 1978 after defeating Muhammad Ali via split decision, in what was considered one of the biggest upsets in boxing history.  Spinks was later stripped of the WBC title for facing Ali in an unapproved rematch seven months later, which he lost by unanimous decision.

Samuel Peter | Samuel Okon Peter (born September 6, 1980) is a Nigerian-American professional boxer who held the WBC heavyweight title in 2008.  He rose to prominence in his early career following a string of knockout wins, at a time when the titles of the heavyweight division were largely fragmented.  In March 2008, Peter won a portion of the world heavyweight championship by knocking out Oleg Maskaev to win the WBC title.  In October, in what would be his only defense of the title, Peter lost to a returning Vitali Klitschko, who stopped him in eight rounds.  Peter is known for his rivalry with the Klitschko brothers, having faced Wladimir twice (in 2005 and 2010) and Vitali once.

1929–30 NHL season | The 1929–30 NHL season was the 13th season of the National Hockey League.  Ten teams played 44 games each.  The Montreal Canadiens upset the heavily favoured Boston Bruins two games to none for the Stanley Cup.
response.summary_1:
- [1] "is a Colombian professional boxer." — Breidis Prescott's nationality (quoted fragment).
- [1] "upset heavily favoured and then-undefeated Olympian Amir Khan" — Prescott defeated Amir Khan in 2008 (quoted fragment).
- [3] After "Khan's disastrous first‑round KO defeat ... at the hands of ... Breidis Prescott" Khan and trainer Jorge Rubio parted ways (paraphrase of passage [3]).
- Bridge entity: Amir Khan (the boxer defeated by Prescott in 2008) — referenced but his nationality is not stated in the supplied passages (missing fact).
- Ambiguity check: Within the provided passages "Amir Khan" refers to the Olympian boxer; no alternative candidate pages for Amir Khan are present in the passages (not ambiguous here).
- Next-hop retrieval targets (prioritized):
  1) Fetch the page "Amir Khan (boxer)" — extract the lead sentence and the infobox fields "Nationality" and "Born (place/date)" (to confirm country/citizenship).
  2) If the boxer page is not available, fetch "Amir Khan (disambiguation)" to locate the boxer entry, then fetch that boxer page and extract the same fields.
  3) Optionally, fetch a reputable boxing database page (e.g., BoxRec entry for Amir Khan) and extract listed nationality/country. 
- No final answer provided here because Amir Khan's nationality is not present or unambiguous in the supplied passages.
hop2_docs: Amir Khan (boxer) | Amir Iqbal Khan (born 8 December 1986) is a British professional boxer.  He is a former unified light-welterweight world champion, having held the WBA (later Super) title from 2009 to 2012, and the IBF title in 2011.  Additionally he held the Commonwealth lightweight title from 2007 to 2008, the WBC Silver welterweight title from 2014 to 2016, and has challenged once for a middleweight world title in 2016.

Amir Amanullah Khan Award | The Amir Amanullah Khan Award is the highest civilian award of Afghanistan.  It was instituted in 2006 and is bestowed by the Government of Afghanistan to Afghan and foreign nationals in recognition of their services to Afghanistan.  The award is named for Amanullah Khan, the Sovereign of the Kingdom of Afghanistan from 1919 to 1929.  The citation on the reverse of the medal states, ""nishan-e dawlati" Ghazi Amir Amanullah Khan", meaning "State Order of Ghazi Amir Amanullah Khan."
Amir Khan Sabri | Hajji Amir Khan Sabri was elected to represent Khost Province in Afghanistan's Wolesi Jirga, the lower house of its National Legislature, in 2005.
Qaleh-ye Amir Khan | Qaleh-ye Amir Khan or Qaleh Amir Khan (Persian: قلعه امير خان‎ ‎ ) may refer to:

Khalid Amir Khan | Khalid Amir Khan is a Pakistani diplomat and politician.  Born on 2 December 1934 to Bilquis Amir and Amir Abdullah Khan, a large landlord from a prominent Pashtun family settled in Sargodha District, Punjab, Pakistan.  Khalid Amir served as ambassador to Hungary, Tajikistan, and Uzbekistan in the 1990s, and played a major role in the politics of the Punjab and especially the Sargodha district.

Amir Khan I | Mir Amir Khan I, Amir Khan I was the title of Mir Abul Wafa the eldest son of Mir Qasim Khan Namkiu.  He was a nobleman in the time of the Mughal emperors Jahangir and Shah Jahan.  At the time of his death he was Subedar or governor of Thatta where he died 1647 aged more than 100 years.

Amir Khan II | Amir Khan II surnamed Mir Miran the son of Khalilullah Khan Yezdi was a nobleman of high rank in the time of the Mughal emperors Shah Jahan and Alamgir and a great favorite of the latter.  He was Subedar or governor of Kabul.  He died at Kabul on the April 28, 1698 and the emperor conferred the title of Amir Khan III on his son.
response.summary_2: Amir Khan is a British professional boxer. Amir Iqbal Khan (born 8 December 1986) is a British professional boxer and a former unified light‑welterweight world champion.
